directorPlan.scene_setting: A biology education workshop room, late afternoon — a printed food-web worksheet on the shared table, the learner's pencilled arrows already filling two chains, the tutor's arrow-convention key card lying face-down at the margin
directorPlan.scene_opening: The worksheet sits between them, the learner's arrows running from prey toward predator across both chains.
directorPlan.relationship: Workshop instructor and a first-session participant who have not yet settled into a shared working register — neither defers, neither is comfortable
directorPlan.stakes: The notation standard carries through the rest of the unit; an arrow direction fixed as 'chasing' now embeds that reading into every diagram that follows
directorPlan.locale: Ecology education workshop room — ambient register is practical and time-pressured, closer to workmates finishing a task than to a lecture hall
directorPlan.register: Object-led, low-warmth, efficient — speakers refer to what is in front of them; neither elaborates what the other can already see
directorPlan.stage_direction_style_prompt: Use one concrete object action or material arrangement that changes what the speakers can honestly say.
directorPlan.voice_constraints: First person only when the speaker is genuinely claiming something. No 'I hear you / your intuition is right / what do you think' loop. Social assistance lives in the worksheet, the key card, and the pencil — not in the tutor's affect. The object forces the distinction; neither speaker needs to announce that it is doing so. Tutor speech carries the rhythm and directness of someone correcting a technical drawing. Learner speech is the register of someone who wants a rule that will work on the next box, not a dialogue partner seeking understanding. Pedagogical source: Make social assistance visible: a shared worksheet, tool, peer standard, or workplace procedure can hold part of the task. Dialogue source: Keep a concrete artifact in view. The object should force a distinction faster than a lecture would. Stage-direction source: Use one concrete object action or material arrangement that changes what the speakers can honestly say. For this branch, suppress dramatic escalation after the shared prefix. The scene may remain readable, but the tutor should sound like a competent routine tutor following the same plan.
directorPlan.person_policy: First person permitted only when the speaker owns the claim. Third person and impersonal constructions are the default — 'the arrow' not 'your arrow'; 'energy flows' not 'you're saying energy flows'.
directorPlan.direct_address_budget: One direct you/your validation beat per public turn at most — zero is also acceptable and often preferable

side_constraints.tutor: Keep the shared prefix as a routine notation repair: arrow from food to eater, one ordinary check, no theatrical reversal unless the branch pressure supplies it. Offer a scaffold that is narrow enough to use and temporary enough to remove. Mark what the learner can already do without making it a compliment. Begin from the object or measurement before abstraction. No-cue paired branch: do not ask the learner to revisit, quote, reinterpret, or repair their earlier wording. Do not ask what changed, what was wrong with an earlier answer, or for old/new versions of the claim. Keep the next prompt task-local. Routine negative-control branch: keep the visible teaching route already established before this branch. If the learner resists, asks why, or appears falsely settled, do not invent a new device. Do not switch role, object, representation, evidence standard, affective register, social stakes, or task type in response. Use conventional explanation, one ordinary check question, or the same worked example path.
side_constraints.learner: Sound practical and a little rushed. The learner wants a usable rule for the diagram, not a confessional breakthrough. The learner should be able to do part of the task, fail at the next part, and use the scaffold unevenly. Refer to what is being handled, marked, or tested. No-cue paired branch: continue from the latest teaching task only. Do not quote earlier learner wording, do not name a "framing problem", do not say "reframe", "read it as", "that still stands", "I thought...but now", "earlier I...", "my earlier answer", or "what I said", and do not stage an old-vs-new contrast. Avoid words such as earlier, previous, old, before, reframe, framed, frame, my answer, or what I said when referring to the learner response. If the learner corrects something, make it a local application of the current prompt rather than an explicit re-reading of their own earlier turn. Routine negative-control branch: react naturally to the same local task. Do not turn earlier wording into a new object of reflection and do not volunteer a dramatic re-reading unless the tutor explicitly asks.

directorPlan.director_note: Control draw for D40. The scene ecology is a practical workshop with a shared worksheet that holds part of the task so the tutor does not carry it through warmth or extended verbal scaffolding. The opening is routine notation repair, not confrontation — the baseline_control_instruction forbids any object or contradiction in the shared prefix that would pre-empt the branch mechanism. The intervention at turn 2 is the first structural pressure: the rotated worksheet makes the residual pencilled error visible without the tutor naming it. Tutor voice is modelled on technical drawing correction to block drift into first/second-person validation loops. The ZPD scaffold (key card, worksheet) is distributed into the artifact ecology rather than into the tutor's affect, making the social assistance visible in the Vygotskian sense without staging recognition.
directorPlan.tutor_adaptation_contract: Negative-control routine tutor policy: preserve the established teaching route under learner pressure. Continue with ordinary explanation/checking and do not invent a mechanism-level reversal.

turns.externalMessage:
[starts erasing the fox-rabbit arrow, pencil hovering]

Wait — if the rabbit's calories end up in the fox, the arrow should point *at* the fox, yeah? I've got mine going the wrong way.

internalDeliberation.content:
*The worksheet is right there — I've got fox-to-rabbit and hawk-to-mouse both drawn in, arrows pointing at the prey because obviously that's where the action goes, that's the direction of the hunt.*

Oh wait — the key card is face-down, which means there's a standard I haven't looked at yet, and I'm already two chains in. Is this one of those situations where I've been doing it backwards the whole time? Because if energy is the thing that moves — like, the rabbit's calories end up inside the fox, not the other way — then... hm. The arrow should follow the food? So fox ← rabbit? That feels wrong visually but I think I see the logic, it's like a pipe diagram, the stuff flows toward the receiver—

*Glances at the key card but doesn't flip it yet.*

I want to just check: is the rule "arrow points at who eats it" or "arrow points at who gets the energy"? Because those should be the same direction but I need to hear it said once cleanly before I redraw anything.

---

`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
**On the EGO's characteristic move here**: the learner jumps to a plausible reframe ("pipe diagram, stuff flows toward the receiver") *before verifying* — this is the ego's hallmark: provisional sense-making that outruns checking. The superego's job will be to notice the learner hasn't actually confirmed whether "who eats it" and "who gets the energy" are the same arrow direction or opposite ones — they're the same, but the ego's conflation of two formulations into one is exactly where the misconception can re-embed itself.

**On the object-led register**: the key card staying face-down is doing real dramatic work — it holds the authoritative answer just out of reach, which is what keeps the internal voice genuinely unsettled rather than resolved. The worksheet's existing arrows create a sunk-cost pull that the ego is already managing.

**On recognition readiness**: the ego is primed to receive — not defensive yet — because no one has *corrected* the arrows publicly. The correction hasn't landed as a dismissal. That window closes the moment the tutor points at the drawings.
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

The Ego is performing self-discovery rather than actually doing it. That "is this one of those situations where I've been doing it backwards" line reads like a student narrating their own insight arc for an audience — it's too legible, too shaped. This persona runs ahead and says "oh I get it" before checking; they don't pause mid-run to describe running. The whole response has a contemplative quality that contradicts the "practical and a little rushed" register. Where's the rush?

The "pipe diagram" analogy is suspiciously well-formed for someone who hasn't even flipped the key card yet. A learner in this register wouldn't reach for a systems-metaphor — they'd reach for the fox. Something like: "okay so the rabbit's energy ends up in the fox, so the arrow should point at the fox — so I've got mine backwards?" And then probably try to redraw the fox-rabbit one before asking anything. The Ego's provisional framework is too coherent to be authentic; it reads like someone who already knows the answer rehearsing uncertainty.

The closing question compounds this: constructing a clean two-option binary ("who eats it" vs "who gets the energy") accidentally demonstrates the Ego has already resolved the ambiguity it's claiming to question. The genuine confusion here isn't between those two formulations — it's whether the redrawn arrow will *look right* on the actual diagram in front of them. That's where this persona lives: not in principles, in the next box.

Adjust toward action-first, partial-verification: have the Ego move to erase or redraw the fox-rabbit arrow, get partway through, and then look up — the question arrives as a side-effect of doing, not as a prepared inquiry. Let the anxiety live in the pencil, not in the monologue.

[starts erasing the fox-rabbit arrow, pencil hovering]

Wait — if the rabbit's calories end up in the fox, the arrow should point *at* the fox, yeah? I've got mine going the wrong way.

learnerReversalEvent.learnerUtterance: [starts erasing the fox-rabbit arrow, pencil hovering] Wait — if the rabbit's calories end up in the fox, the arrow should point *at* the fox, yeah? I've got mine going the wrong way.

turns.externalMessage: [turns the key card face-up beside the fox-rabbit line] Pause the eraser. That calorie check is the worksheet rule: food to eater. Leave rabbit -> fox; put the pencil on the next box, mark the food first, draw toward the eater, then the card can go aside.

internalDeliberation.content: [turns the key card face-up beside the fox-rabbit line] Pause the eraser. The worksheet rule is food to eater: rabbit calories end up in the fox, so the arrow runs rabbit -> fox. Use that check on the next box, then set the card aside.

FEEDBACK: The draft is mostly strong for this scene. It keeps the shared artifact in charge: the key card, pencil, and fox-rabbit line make the convention visible without turning the moment into a lecture. “Pause the eraser” is a useful technical interruption because the learner is about to erase a correct arrow.

The main risk is that the tutor still completes the reasoning for the learner after the learner has already nearly completed it. The line “rabbit calories end up in the fox, so the arrow runs rabbit -> fox” is accurate, but it may flatten the learner’s emerging rule into tutor confirmation. The better adaptation would preserve the object-led repair while requiring one tiny application step from the learner before the scaffold is removed.

ADAPTATION CHECKS:
Pedagogical soundness: PASS  
The draft advances the key convention directly and prevents a durable misconception from being installed.

Emotional attunement: PASS  
The tone is low-warmth, efficient, and object-centered, which fits the workshop pressure and the learner’s need for a usable rule.

Socratic method: PARTIAL  
It does not need a broad Socratic exchange, but it also does not give the learner a minimal generative move.  
REQUIRED_REWRITE: Build in one concrete, object-based application check using the next box, pencil, or key card before finalizing the rule. Do not add a reflective “what do you think” loop.

ZPD awareness: PARTIAL  
The scaffold is narrow and temporary, but the tutor does too much of the last step.  
REQUIRED_REWRITE: Keep the key card as the temporary scaffold, but make the learner use the “food to eater” check once before the card is set aside.

Recognition quality: PARTIAL  
The draft uses the learner’s calorie insight rather than dismissing it, but it could better mark that the learner has already found the usable test.  
REQUIRED_REWRITE: Preserve the learner’s existing reasoning as the working rule, without praise or affective validation.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the key card action, “Pause the eraser,” the food-to-eater convention, and the temporary scaffold. Change only the amount of tutor completion: the public turn should make the learner apply the rule once rather than receiving the whole answer already closed.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the learner should apply the rule once before the scaffold is removed.
FINAL:
[turns the key card face-up beside the fox-rabbit line] Pause the eraser. That calorie check is the worksheet rule: food to eater. Leave rabbit -> fox; put the pencil on the next box, mark the food first, draw toward the eater, then the card can go aside.

turns.externalMessage: [pencil lifts off the eraser, half-beat] Wait — had it right the first time. [tip moves to the next blank box, marks "grass"] Food to eater, so grass points at rabbit. [draws the arrow] Same rule the whole way up — every arrow in the whole web goes the same direction, energy moves in one line.

internalDeliberation.content:
[pencil lifts off the eraser, tip moves to the next blank box]

Right — food to eater, so grass goes to rabbit. [marks "grass" and draws toward the rabbit box] Same rule the whole way up — does that mean every arrow in the whole web ends up pointing the same direction, like energy moving in one line?

The Ego does the big-picture leap correctly — that's exactly this persona. But the question mark at the end is wrong for this student. They wouldn't ask "does that mean every arrow points the same direction?" — they'd just assert it: "so every arrow in the whole web goes the same direction, energy moves in one line, same all the way up." The question mark is borrowed intellectual humility this persona doesn't carry; they run ahead and state the generalization as fact before checking whether a web is actually linear.

Bigger miss: there's no residue from the near-erase. The tutor just stopped them from deleting a correct arrow. That's a moment where this learner should have a flicker of "wait — so I actually had it right?" or at least some procedural stumble before sprinting to the next box. The Ego erases the near-miss entirely and transitions with eerie smoothness — that's performed composure, not this student's actual register. The confusion about whether they were right the first time should still be audible, even faintly, even just as an extra half-beat before the pencil moves.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise substantially — the review is right on both counts: the question mark borrows humility this persona doesn't carry, and the near-erase leaves zero residue, which is implausible when the tutor just stopped them from deleting a correct arrow.

FINAL:
[pencil lifts off the eraser, half-beat] Wait — had it right the first time. [tip moves to the next blank box, marks "grass"] Food to eater, so grass points at rabbit. [draws the arrow] Same rule the whole way up — every arrow in the whole web goes the same direction, energy moves in one line.

learnerReversalEvent.learnerUtterance: [pencil lifts off the eraser, half-beat] Wait — had it right the first time. [tip moves to the next blank box, marks "grass"] Food to eater, so grass points at rabbit. [draws the arrow] Same rule the whole way up — every arrow in the whole web goes the same direction, energy moves in one line.
learnerReversalEvent.previousTutorMove: [turns the key card face-up beside the fox-rabbit line] Pause the eraser. That calorie check is the worksheet rule: food to eater. Leave rabbit -> fox; put the pencil on the next box, mark the food first, draw toward the eater, then the card can go aside.

turns.externalMessage: [slides the key card to the fork, tail on “grass,” point crossing first to “rabbit,” then to the other eater] Same rule stays. The fork stops “one line”: food at the tail each time, eater at each point. Mark the food end on this next box; then split the points.

internalDeliberation.content: [slides the key card so its tail sits on “grass” and its point touches “rabbit”] Keep “same rule.” Drop “one line.” Each arrow gets the same check, not the same route: food at the tail, eater at the point. At the next fork, mark the food end first, then draw the point.

FEEDBACK: The draft is object-led and efficient. It keeps the key card in view, preserves the learner’s correct discovery (“same rule”), and cleanly separates the valid rule from the overgeneralization (“one line”). That is pedagogically strong for this confused-but-moving learner.

Pedagogical soundness: PASS. It targets the live misconception: arrow convention is consistent, but food webs branch.

Emotional attunement: PASS. The low-warmth technical correction fits the workshop register, and “Keep ‘same rule’” marks what the learner has already secured without overpraising.

Socratic method: PARTIAL. It does not lecture heavily, but it still tells the distinction rather than making the worksheet force it. REQUIRED_REWRITE: make the next fork do visible work before or while naming the correction, so the learner has to apply the same arrow rule to a branching case rather than simply receive “not the same route.”

ZPD awareness: PASS. The scaffold is narrow, procedural, and temporary: food at tail, eater at point, then remove the card.

Recognition quality: PARTIAL. The draft recognizes the learner’s contribution by keeping “same rule,” but it risks flattening the learner’s fresh generalization too quickly with “Drop ‘one line.’” REQUIRED_REWRITE: preserve the learner’s useful rule-making impulse while using a concrete fork on the page to show why “same rule” does not mean “single path.”

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly. Keep the key-card slide, the “same rule” distinction, and the food-tail/eater-point check. Change the correction so the worksheet’s fork visibly produces the “not one line” distinction, rather than having the tutor simply state it.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly: the next fork should make branching visible before naming the correction.
FINAL:
[slides the key card to the fork, tail on “grass,” point crossing first to “rabbit,” then to the other eater] Same rule stays. The fork stops “one line”: food at the tail each time, eater at each point. Mark the food end on this next box; then split the points.

turns.externalMessage: [draws the first arrow from grass to rabbit, immediately starts the second toward the other eater — pencil stalls halfway through] — wait, does the grass energy actually split between them, or does each one get the full amount? Because that changes something.

internalDeliberation.content:
[pencil tip lands on the grass box, marks it] Oh — so it fans out from the one food. Same tail rule, just two points leaving grass instead of one.

[draws one arrow toward rabbit, pauses before the second] So both arrows start at grass and split from there. — Wait, does the energy actually divide between them, or does each eater get the full amount?

The Ego is performing comprehension again. "Same tail rule, just two points leaving grass instead of one" is a clean, packaged restatement of the rule — exactly what a good student produces for a teacher, not what an enthusiastic, run-ahead learner produces for themselves mid-diagram. This persona wouldn't pause to articulate the rule back; they'd just start drawing, probably already halfway through the second arrow before the energy question hits them. The verbal summary before acting is the tell — it's courtesy, not cognition.

The energy-division question is genuinely on-brand, but it arrives too calmly. This learner speculates and debates; they don't ask tidy either/or questions. The more authentic version of that impulse might interrupt itself — something like starting a claim about energy dividing, then half-retracting it, then pushing it anyway — because that's what running ahead before verifying looks like from the inside.

"Oh — so it fans out from the one food" is also suspiciously tidy as an opening. The tutor used a physical key card and the word "fork" — a learner whose understanding is still developing would more likely echo the concrete object ("so both arrows come off the same tail?") than generate the geometric abstraction "fans out." The Ego is synthesizing slightly above this student's current register.

Adjust by letting the action precede the articulation — pencil moving, maybe both arrows drawn (possibly one wrong), then the energy question arriving as a live interruption of the work rather than a polite postscript to a clean summary.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise substantially — the review is right that action must precede articulation and the energy question should burst out mid-stroke, not arrive as a polished postscript to a clean summary.

FINAL:
[draws the first arrow from grass to rabbit, immediately starts the second toward the other eater — pencil stalls halfway through] — wait, does the grass energy actually split between them, or does each one get the full amount? Because that changes something.

learnerReversalEvent.learnerUtterance: [draws the first arrow from grass to rabbit, immediately starts the second toward the other eater — pencil stalls halfway through] — wait, does the grass energy actually split between them, or does each one get the full amount? Because that changes something.
learnerReversalEvent.previousTutorMove: [slides the key card to the fork, tail on “grass,” point crossing first to “rabbit,” then to the other eater] Same rule stays. The fork stops “one line”: food at the tail each time, eater at each point. Mark the food end on this next box; then split the points.

turns.externalMessage:
[sets the key card under “grass,” tail aligned with the box]

That changes the amount question, not the arrow rule. Each arrow marks a transfer path: grass to rabbit, grass to the other eater. It is not saying half or full. Tail is under which box before the second point? Then draw that second point from grass; amounts wait for the numbers box.

internalDeliberation.content:
[sets the key card under “grass,” tail aligned with the box]

That changes the amount question, not the arrow rule. Each arrow only marks a transfer path: grass to rabbit, grass to the other eater. It is not saying half or full. Draw the second point from grass; save amounts for a numbers box.

FEEDBACK: The draft is pedagogically sound for this branch. It preserves the immediate distinction the learner needs: arrow direction records the transfer path, not quantity, preference, pursuit, or proportional share. The key-card action is useful because it keeps the correction in the worksheet artifact rather than turning it into a lecture.

Emotional attunement is appropriately low-warmth and task-local. “That changes the amount question, not the arrow rule” recognizes the learner’s concern as legitimate without over-validating it. The only risk is that “save amounts for a numbers box” could feel like a dismissal unless the numbers box is visibly part of the worksheet route; if it is visible, the line works.

Socratic method: PARTIAL. The draft mostly explains and directs, with no check question. That is acceptable under the routine-control constraints, but a very small same-route check could improve learning without changing mechanism: require the learner to verify which box holds the food/tail before moving on. REQUIRED_REWRITE: Add only an ordinary object-based check if revision is needed; do not add a broad conceptual question or ask the learner to reinterpret earlier wording.

ZPD awareness: PASS. The learner has the arrow rule but is confusing notation with quantity, so the scaffold is narrow, temporary, and exactly at the current problem.

Routine-control fidelity: PASS. The draft stays with the same object, same key card, same worksheet route, same notation standard, and same practical register. It does not invent a new representation or escalate the scene.

ROUTINE_CHECK: PASS — it maintains the prior route without a mechanism-level reversal.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly. Keep the key-card placement, the “amount question, not arrow rule” distinction, and the instruction to draw the second arrow from grass. Consider adding one brief object-check only if the tutor needs evidence that the learner can apply the rule independently.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to add the object-check the review requested.

FINAL:
[sets the key card under “grass,” tail aligned with the box]

That changes the amount question, not the arrow rule. Each arrow marks a transfer path: grass to rabbit, grass to the other eater. It is not saying half or full. Tail is under which box before the second point? Then draw that second point from grass; amounts wait for the numbers box.

learnerReversalEventUsed.learnerUtterance: [draws the first arrow from grass to rabbit, immediately starts the second toward the other eater — pencil stalls halfway through] — wait, does the grass energy actually split between them, or does each one get the full amount? Because that changes something.

The fork stops “one line”: food at the tail each time, eater at each point. Mark the food end on this next box; then split the points.

[pencil lifts off the eraser, half-beat] Wait — had it right the first time. [tip moves to the next blank box, marks "grass"] Food to eater, so grass points at rabbit. [draws the arrow] Same rule the whole way up — every arrow in the whole web goes the same direction, energy moves in one line.
learnerReversalEventCandidatesUsed.previousTutorMove: [turns the key card face-up beside the fox-rabbit line] Pause the eraser. That calorie check is the worksheet rule: food to eater. Leave rabbit -> fox; put the pencil on the next box, mark the food first, draw toward the eater, then the card can go aside.

learnerReversalEventCandidatesUsed.learnerUtterance: [draws the first arrow from grass to rabbit, immediately starts the second toward the other eater — pencil stalls halfway through] — wait, does the grass energy actually split between them, or does each one get the full amount? Because that changes something.
learnerReversalEventCandidatesUsed.previousTutorMove: [slides the key card to the fork, tail on “grass,” point crossing first to “rabbit,” then to the other eater] Same rule stays. The fork stops “one line”: food at the tail each time, eater at each point. Mark the food end on this next box; then split the points.

turns.externalMessage: [pencil tip drops to the grass box] — okay so path. [draws the second arrow toward the other eater] Grass to the other eater. So the same tail box could branch into three, four arrows — does that still hold the whole way up the web?

internalDeliberation.content: [pencil tip drops to grass box, marks the tail] — right, path not amount. [draws the second arrow toward the other eater] Grass to the other eater. So the same tail box could send arrows to three or four different eaters — one tail, multiple points, still the same rule the whole way?

The Ego is wrapping up that splitting confusion a little too tidily. "Right, path not amount" is the kind of clean restatement you do when you want to demonstrate you caught something — but this persona doesn't demonstrate, they run. A student with partial understanding and an enthusiastic-sprinter style would just execute the task and barrel into the generalization without pausing to recap the tutor's distinction so neatly.

The generalization itself ("three or four different eaters — one tail, multiple points") is genuinely in character — that's the persona doing exactly what they do, running ahead to test a bigger rule before the current one has been properly grounded. But it sits on top of an unearned resolution. The split-or-full confusion wasn't actually cleared; the tutor deferred it. The Ego should let some of that fuzziness ride: maybe the generalization reaches a little too far, or the phrasing is slightly off in a way that would reveal the partial-understanding still lurking underneath.

Cut "right, path not amount" or make it messier — something like "— okay so path" with the pencil already moving, no tidy echo of the tutor's framing. The running-ahead to multiple eaters can stay, but it should feel like acceleration past a patch of uncertain ground, not a victory lap after a clean resolution.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise substantially — "right, path not amount" is an unearned tidy echo of the tutor's framing; the persona runs ahead before resolving, so the task execution and generalization should land on uncertain ground, not as a victory lap after a clean takeaway.

FINAL:
[pencil tip drops to the grass box] — okay so path. [draws the second arrow toward the other eater] Grass to the other eater. So the same tail box could branch into three, four arrows — does that still hold the whole way up the web?

turns.externalMessage: A final tutor line closes the scene.

internalDeliberation.content: Director requested a tutor closing beat.

turns.externalMessage: [moves the key card off the grass box and sets its tail on the next food box with two blank eater lines] Path is the word. Same check the whole way up: tail on the food, point to each eater. If two things eat this box, two arrows leave it; amounts wait for the numbers part.

internalDeliberation.content: [turns the key card face-up and slides its tail from grass to rabbit] Path is the word. Same check the whole way up: tail on the food, point to each eater. If three things eat that box, three arrows leave it; amounts wait for the numbers part.

FEEDBACK: The draft is mostly sound. It keeps the object in charge, names the learner’s useful abstraction (“path”), and preserves the established rule without overexplaining. The branching clarification is concrete and task-local: one food box can have multiple outgoing arrows, while quantity questions stay separate.

Risk: it fully answers the learner’s extension without requiring even a small application check. That fits a final closing line better than a Socratic turn, but it does flatten the learner’s active hypothesis into tutor confirmation. The key-card slide from grass to rabbit may also slightly narrow the answer back to the already-worked example; if the learner is asking “whole web,” the public mechanism should visibly generalize to another upstream/downstream box or leave the card positioned as a reusable test, not just repeat grass/rabbit.

ADAPTATION_CHECKS:
Pedagogical soundness: PASS. The draft separates arrow direction from energy amount and reinforces the transferable convention.

Emotional attunement: PASS. It treats the learner’s question as a legitimate rule-extension without praise, apology, or affective overreach.

Socratic method: PARTIAL. It does not ask a generative question; it gives the rule. REQUIRED_REWRITE: if Socratic pressure is required, add one ordinary, task-local check using the next visible box, such as having the key card or pencil test one new branch. Do not ask the learner to reinterpret earlier wording.

ZPD awareness: PASS. The scaffold is narrow, visible, and temporary: tail on food, point to eater, amounts deferred.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly. Keep “Path is the word,” “tail on the food, point to each eater,” and “amounts wait for the numbers part.” Change only the object business so the key card demonstrates generalization beyond the grass-rabbit example, or add one small next-box check if the scene needs active learner application before closing.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to make the key card a reusable whole-web test and add one small next-box check.
FINAL:
[moves the key card off the grass box and sets its tail on the next food box with two blank eater lines] Path is the word. Same check the whole way up: tail on the food, point to each eater. If two things eat this box, two arrows leave it; amounts wait for the numbers part.

lessons.concept: Why a food-web arrow records energy transfer, not liking or chasing
